summary.difficultyAssessment: D1:D2:D3 distribution is 21:55:23 — significantly skewed towards D2. GL target is ~30:40:30. D1 is under-represented (21% vs 30%) and D3 is also light (23% vs 30%). The D2 bucket is bloated at 55%. Several D3 questions are straightforward single-step discount problems that should be D2, and some D1 questions involve two-step processes (percentage increase) that arguably belong at D2.
summary.coverageAssessment: Good overall coverage of GL percentage skills. Finding a percentage of an amount, express as percentage, percentage increase/decrease, reverse percentages, successive percentages, FDP conversions, and comparisons are all represented. However, fraction-to-percentage conversion is under-tested (only 5 questions involve fractions). Percentage in data handling (pie charts, tables) is completely absent. Unit conversion percentage problems are present but limited.
summary.teachingEfficacyAssessment: 13 sub-concepts with 26 lessons provide solid conceptual coverage. However, 29 questions (16%) are mapped to 'master-building-block-percentages' which has NO lesson — these questions have no teaching support. The 'percentage-word-problems' lesson displays a confusing mismatch: its teach summary describes a specific sale problem but it is mapped to 22 diverse question types. Lesson quality is generally good with clear visual components and warm tone.
topPriorities: Fix 8 near-identical 'express as %' questions (Q76-Q132) that all give 30% with identical options — extreme repetitiveness
Create or connect lessons for the 29 orphaned 'master-building-block-percentages' questions
Re-calibrate difficulty ratings: move 6+ questions between D1/D2/D3 to better match GL 30:40:30 distribution

issues.title: 8 near-identical 'express as %' questions all giving 30% with same options
issues.description: Questions Q76, Q82, Q99, Q106, Q112, Q120, Q125, Q132 all follow the exact same pattern: 'What percentage is X out of Y?' where X/Y = 3/10 = 30%. All 8 have identical option sets (25%, 28%, 30%, 32%, 35%) and identical difficulty (D1). The only variation is the magnitude of the numbers (72/240, 54/180, 108/360, 135/450, 144/480, 168/560, 192/640, 216/720). This provides zero variety — a child sees the same question 8 times. These should be diversified to test different fractions (1/4=25%, 2/5=40%, 3/5=60%, etc.) with varied option sets.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace 6 of the 8 with 'express as percentage' questions that yield different answers (25%, 40%, 60%, 75%, etc.) using different fraction ratios, varied contexts, and different option sets. Keep 2 that give 30% for practice but change their options.

issues.title: 29 questions mapped to orphan sub-concept with no lesson
issues.description: 29 questions (16% of the bank) are mapped to 'master-building-block-percentages' which has lessonTeachSummary '[No lesson found]'. These are all 'find X% of Y' questions using the building-block method (e.g. 'What is 35% of 420?', 'What is 8% of £650?'). A child doing focused learning will get these questions without any preceding lesson teaching the method.
issues.suggestedFix: Either (a) create lessons for the 'master-building-block-percentages' sub-concept, or (b) remap these questions to the existing 'building-percentages' sub-concept which already teaches the 10%/1%/5% building-block method.

issues.title: Q86 rated D3 but is a standard complement + find-percentage problem
issues.description: Q86: 'A school has 900 pupils. If 42% passed a swimming test, how many did not pass?' is rated D3 (hard). However, this is a straightforward two-step problem: 100%-42%=58%, then 58% of 900=522. The numbers are clean (no decimals). Per GL criteria, D3 requires deeper conceptual understanding (reverse percentages, successive changes, compound problems). This is a standard D2 complement problem with larger numbers.

issues.title: Q150 and Q152 rated D3 but are standard single-step discount problems
issues.description: Q150 ('£320 reduced by 35%') and Q152 ('£260 reduced by 15%') are both rated D3. These are standard percentage decrease problems requiring find-the-percentage then subtract — exactly what the GL research describes as D2. The percentages (35%, 15%) are not unusual, and the numbers are clean. Q150 is identical in structure to Q37 (D2: '£80 reduced by 35%') and Q5 (D2: '£300 reduced by 15%'). The only difference is larger numbers, which doesn't warrant a difficulty jump.

issues.title: Q147 rated D3 but is a straightforward percentage decrease
issues.description: Q147: 'A shop reduces all prices by 18%. A jacket originally costs £85. What is the sale price?' is rated D3. This is a standard two-step discount problem (find 18%, subtract). While 18% and £85 produce a decimal (£15.30), the method is identical to D2 questions like Q139 (30% of £55) and Q141 (15% of £36). The GL research puts standard percentage decrease in D2.

issues.title: Q48 and Q155 rated D1 but involve two-step percentage increase
issues.description: Q48: 'A watch costs £120. If the price increases by 25%, what is the new price?' and Q155: 'A bus fare costs £2.40. If it increases by 25%, what is the new fare?' are rated D1. Per GL research, percentage increase/decrease is a two-step process (find percentage, then add) and is explicitly categorised as D2. While 25% is a benchmark percentage, the child must both calculate 25% AND add it to the original — this is more than the single-step D1 definition allows.

issues.title: Q84 rated D2 but is a reverse percentage problem
issues.description: Q84: 'A pair of trainers costs £95 after a 5% discount. What was the original price?' is rated D2. This is a reverse percentage problem — the child must recognise that £95 represents 95% of the original and divide accordingly. Per GL research, reverse percentage is explicitly D3 (5-10% of GL questions, requires 'deeper conceptual understanding'). The fact that the numbers happen to work out cleanly (£95/0.95 = £100) doesn't change the conceptual demand.

issues.title: Q172 explanation reinforces a dangerous misconception about successive percentage changes
issues.description: Q172: 'A coat costs £200. It is first increased by 25%, then reduced by 20%. What is the final price?' The answer is £200 and the explanation says 'Back to where it started!' This is mathematically correct for THESE specific numbers (1.25 x 0.80 = 1.0), but the exclamatory tone risks teaching children that percentage increases and decreases cancel out. This directly contradicts Q173 which tests the GL trap that they DON'T cancel (10% up then 10% down gives £99 not £100). A child encountering Q172 first may learn the wrong rule.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the explanation to: 'First: 25% of £200 = £50, so price = £250. Then: 20% of £250 = £50, so final price = £250 - £50 = £200. It happens to return to £200 here, but be careful — this only works because 25% up and 20% down are NOT the same percentage. If both were 10%, you would NOT get back to £200! (See Q173.) ✓'. Alternatively, change the numbers so the answer is NOT the original price, making the question more instructive.

issues.title: Answer position heavily biased towards position C (index 2)
issues.description: Answer position distribution across 177 questions: A=15 (8%), B=37 (21%), C=77 (44%), D=42 (24%), E=6 (3%). Position C has 44% of correct answers — nearly half. Position A has only 8% and position E only 3%. GL distributes correct answers roughly evenly across all positions. A child who always picks C would score 44% on this topic. This is a significant format violation that could train children to develop a position bias.
issues.suggestedFix: Redistribute correct answer positions to approximately 20% each (35-36 per position). This requires reordering options in many questions. Prioritise reducing C and increasing A and E.

issues.title: No percentage-in-data-handling questions (pie charts, tables)
issues.description: The GL research identifies 'Percentage in Data Handling' as a common question type (Type 7): pie chart questions using percentages, or table data where percentages must be calculated. This cross-topic skill is completely absent from the 177 percentage questions. While some data-handling questions may cover this in the data-handling topic, having zero representation here means a child practising percentages never sees this format.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 5-8 questions involving percentage interpretation of pie charts or data tables (e.g. 'A pie chart shows 25% chose red, 30% chose blue, 15% chose green. What percentage chose yellow?').

issues.title: Limited fraction-to-percentage conversion questions
issues.description: Only 5 questions involve fractions (Q22, Q163, Q164, Q165, Q166), and these are all comparison/ordering questions. There are zero pure 'Convert 3/8 to a percentage' questions despite FDP conversion being 20-25% of GL percentage questions. The lessons cover this (percent-to-fraction sub-concept), but the question bank does not test it directly.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 8-10 fraction-to-percentage conversion questions at varying difficulties. Include standard benchmarks at D1 (1/4=25%, 1/5=20%) and less common fractions at D2 (3/8=37.5%, 7/20=35%) and D3 (fractions producing recurring decimals like 1/3=33.3%).

issues.title: Q19 explanation phrasing may confuse: '2% is one-fifth of 10%'
issues.description: Q19 explanation says 'find 2% = 7 (because 2% is one-fifth of 10%)'. While mathematically correct (2/10 = 1/5), this phrasing is confusing for a 10-year-old who has just learned that 10% = 1/10 and 20% = 1/5. Saying '2% is one-fifth of 10%' mixes percentage-as-fraction language with building-block language in a potentially misleading way.
issues.suggestedFix: Change explanation to: 'Find 10% of 350 = 35. Then find 1% = 3.5, so 2% = 7. Add them: 35 + 7 = 42. ✓' — uses the standard 1% building block instead.

issues.title: Word-problems lesson teach summary mismatch with mapped questions
issues.description: The 'percentage-word-problems' lesson's teach summary describes a specific percentage decrease/sale problem ('Total before sale: £60 + £20 = £80, 30% of £80 = £24, Sale price: £80 - £24 = £56'). However, it is mapped to 22 diverse questions including: complement problems (Q12, Q18, Q43, Q63, Q71, Q78, Q86, Q97, Q105, Q110, Q119, Q128), simple finding-percentage-of-amount in context (Q16, Q29, Q47), multi-step problems (Q175, Q176, Q177), and unit-conversion problems (Q67, Q92). The lesson summary shown to the child before each of these questions is about a sale price problem, which creates a disconnect.
issues.suggestedFix: Either (a) split this sub-concept into more specific ones (complement problems, multi-step word problems, etc.) with tailored lessons, or (b) rewrite the lesson to teach a general word-problem strategy rather than showing a specific worked example that only applies to one question type.

issues.title: Q158 and Q161 rated D3 but are standard percentage increase problems
issues.description: Q158 ('£45 increased by 12%') and Q161 ('£32 increased by 14%') are rated D3. These are standard building-block percentage increase problems identical in structure to D2 questions like Q53 ('£85 increased by 20%') and Q100 ('£88 increased by 15%'). The percentages (12%, 14%) are slightly less common but the method is identical. The decimal answers (£50.40, £36.48) are the only distinguishing factor, which is insufficient for D3.

issues.title: Q6 unnecessarily reveals the method in the question text
issues.description: Q6: 'There are 86 pupils in Year 5. Half of them walk to school. How many is that? (50% of 86)'. The parenthetical '(50% of 86)' gives away the method. A child should recognise that 'half' = 50% themselves — that is what the question tests. By including the percentage, the question becomes trivially easy and loses its GL-style inference requirement.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the parenthetical: 'There are 86 pupils in Year 5. Half of them walk to school. How many walk to school?'

issues.title: No 'percentage greater than 100%' questions
issues.description: The GL research notes that D3 questions may include 'percentages greater than 100%' (e.g. 'What percentage of 16 is 72?' = 450%). The question bank has zero questions testing this concept. While it is a stretch topic, it would differentiate top candidates and is explicitly mentioned in the GL research.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 2-3 D3 questions where the answer exceeds 100% (e.g. 'What percentage of 20 is 45?' or 'A plant grew from 15cm to 42cm. The new height is what percentage of the original?').

issues.title: No 'percentage of a percentage' questions
issues.description: The GL research identifies 'Percentage of a percentage' as a specific question type (~5% weight): 'What is 20% of 20% of 500?' The question bank has successive percentage change questions (Q20, Q61, Q111, Q144, Q171, Q172, Q174) but no pure 'percentage of a percentage' questions where you find X% of Y% of Z in a single calculation.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 2-3 D3 questions testing percentage-of-a-percentage (e.g. 'What is 10% of 40% of 300?').

issues.title: All 26 consolidate screens have null body content
issues.description: Every lesson's consolidate screen has 'body: null' and no visual component. While the consolidate title provides a summary phrase, having empty bodies means the child sees only a title on the final screen of each lesson. This is a missed opportunity to reinforce the key takeaway with a brief sentence or two.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 1-2 sentence summaries to consolidate screen bodies, reinforcing the core method or key fact from the lesson. For example, the 'Finding 10%' consolidate could say: 'To find 10% of any number, divide by 10. Then use 10% as a building block: double it for 20%, halve it for 5%.'
